title: A Heart for God's Word
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Heart for God’s Word - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 119_1-8.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Preach Psalm 119?
subpoints: The series was planned for about two years but the pastor was intimidated by its scope
23 sermons planned — 8 verses AM, 8 verses PM — through the entire Psalm
The goal: a soul-stirring, heart-strengthening, spiritually revitalizing study
Psalm 119 sits at the center of the Bible — the Psalm of a man's passionate heart for God's Word

outline.title: The Subject of Psalm 119: The Word of God and the God of the Word
subpoints: Scripture is mentioned nearly 180 times in 175 verses — only one verse lacks a direct reference
Eight synonyms for the Word of God: law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, and two words for 'word'
Each term is used approximately 20-25 times — showing exhaustive, deliberate treatment
The Psalm speaks not only about the Word of God but addresses God Himself some 275 times

outline.title: The Structure: An Acrostic Masterpiece
subpoints: An acrostic poem in which each group of 8 verses begins with a letter of the Hebrew alphabet
22 stanzas — one for each of the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet — 176 verses total
The acrostic represents the A through Z of the subject — a complete, exhaustive tribute
As Christ is the Alpha and Omega, so the Psalm presents the fullness of God's Word

outline.title: The Sufficiency and Centrality of Scripture
subpoints: The Reformation elevated the Scriptures by placing a pulpit with an open Bible at the center
Sola Scriptura: Scripture alone is sufficient for all matters of faith and life
The Word of God is more up-to-date than tomorrow's news — we may be out of touch with it
It was always the preaching of the whole counsel of God that brought revival, not performance

outline.title: The History and Context of Psalm 119
subpoints: Likely a post-exilic Psalm, written in a context of heavy personal persecution
The Psalmist's enemies persecuted, trapped, and sought to destroy him
Yet in every verse his response is to turn to the Word of God for strength and hope
Great Christians throughout history — Spurgeon, Calvin, Augustine, Thomas Manton — were awed by this Psalm

summary:
In this opening Sunday morning sermon of a new series on Psalm 119, Pastor Tito Lyro introduces what he calls the "Mount Everest of the Psalms" — the 176-verse acrostic masterpiece that stands at the heart of the Bible. He candidly admits to having planned and feared this series for about two years before beginning it, citing the Psalm's sheer length, the enormous wealth of scholarly commentary on it, and the inadequacy he felt in approaching something that humbled Augustine, Calvin, and Spurgeon alike.

The sermon identifies the dual subject of the Psalm: it is about the Word of God (referenced nearly 180 times through eight Hebrew synonyms — law, testimonies, precepts, statutes, commandments, judgments, and two words for "word") and about the God of the Word (addressed directly approximately 275 times). The poem is structured as a Hebrew acrostic — 22 stanzas of 8 verses each, one stanza for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet — presenting what the pastor describes as the "A through Z" of Holy Scripture, a complete and exhaustive tribute to its subject.

Pastor Lyro connects the Psalm to the Reformation principle of Sola Scriptura, noting that Calvin ordered the altar to be removed from the center of the sanctuary and replaced with a pulpit bearing an open Bible. The Psalm calls for Scripture alone to hold the central place in the heart of every believer. He closes by noting that the Psalm was written in a context of fierce personal persecution — yet its author in verse after verse turns not to his enemies or his circumstances but to the Word of God. It is this passion that the series hopes to cultivate in the congregation.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 119:1-8

If you're able, please remain standing for the reading of God's Word. I'm going to read Psalm 119, verses 1 through 8. As I was thinking about this series, I considered the possibility of reading the entire Psalm together for the first sermon just to have the whole experience. I decided not to do that. We're going to read verses 1 through 8 this morning. This is the perfect, inspired, and inerrant word of our Lord.

"Blessed are the undefiled in the way, who walk in the law of the Lord. Blessed are those who keep His testimonies, who seek Him with the whole heart. They also do no iniquity; they walk in His ways. You have commanded us to keep Your precepts diligently. Oh, that my ways were directed to keep Your statutes! Then I would not be ashamed when I look into all Your commandments. I will praise You with uprightness of heart, when I learn Your righteous judgments. I will keep Your statutes; O do not forsake me utterly."

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that you open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning your law from this passage and from this Psalm. We pray that as we consider the entire Psalm through the year, weeks and months to come, that you would revive us to love you and to love your word. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: Why Psalm 119 Now?

I have been planning and considering a series on Psalm 119 for a while now — probably two years. But I have been very scared of it. I wasn't sure if it could become a monotonous series because of the theme, or whether I would actually have the ability to get my mind around it to preach it. And nothing scared me more than the amount of material written on Psalm 119 — to get a good sample of what people have thought about it takes an enormous amount of time.

But what overcame my laziness is this: I thought a series of 23 sermons on this Psalm — one Sunday morning, one Sunday afternoon, 8 verses at a time — would help us grow in our passion and love for God's Word. My hope is that this series will be soul-stirring, life-changing, heart-strengthening, spiritually revitalizing and refreshing — that God would use it to revive us as a church and as individuals. And I hope and pray God will do that for my own heart and for all of our hearts as we go through Psalm 119 together.

## The Position of Psalm 119 in the Bible

Many Bibles have Psalm 119 very near the center. In fact, technically Psalm 117 is the middle chapter of the Bible — 594 chapters before it and 594 after it. But Psalm 118 is the closest to the center, and right there, at the very heart of God's Word, we find Psalm 119. It is fitting that at the other actual center of Scripture we find a Psalm that speaks of the centrality of the Scriptures in everyday life — a Psalm of a man's passionate heart for God's Word.

James Boice, in his preface to Calvin's sermons on Psalm 119, writes: "When the Protestant Reformation took place in the 16th century, and the truths of the Bible which had long been obscured by the traditions of the medieval church again became known, there was an immediate elevation of the Scriptures in Protestant services. John Calvin in particular carried this out with thoroughness, ordering that the altar — long the center of the Latin Mass — be removed from the churches, and that a pulpit with a Bible open be placed in the center of the building. This was not to be on one side of the room but at the center, where every line of the architecture would carry the gaze of the people to that book."

I hope we can consider that as we think about a new building — that the architecture would tell a story about what we believe concerning the Scriptures, their centrality in the worship of the Lord.

This Psalm calls for Scripture alone to have the central and primary place in all of our hearts and lives. It embodies the Reformation motto of *Sola Scriptura* — Scripture alone is sufficient for all matters of faith and life. It was always the Word being proclaimed in a worship service shaped by the Word of God that brought revival to the church. Not performance. Not entertainment. It was the preaching of the whole counsel of God, verse by verse, book by book, that truly changed lives. Nothing is more relevant for us than the Scriptures. The Scriptures are more up-to-date than tomorrow's news. We don't need to make the Bible relevant — it is relevant. We may be out of touch with it, but it is always relevant.

## The Subject of the Psalm

What I want to get across today is that the subject of this Psalm is the Word of God and the God of the Word. Not only about the Word of God, but also about the God of the Word. This is one of the few times where virtually all commentators agree on the theme of a chapter. Scripture is indisputably the main subject — mentioned nearly 180 times in 175 verses. There is only one verse in the whole Psalm that doesn't refer to the Bible directly by a synonym. Even just looking at the first eight verses we read, every verse has a reference to the Bible.

The author uses **eight terms** to refer to the Bible, each used approximately 20 to 25 times:

1. **Law** (Hebrew: *Torah*) — not just the Ten Commandments, but all the teaching and instruction of the Lord, representing the whole Bible
2. **Testimonies** — things God is testifying about Himself and His Word; because they come from God, they are believable
3. **Precepts** — declarations of what God expects us to do
4. **Statutes** — from the word for carving or engraving on stone; they are permanent, unchangeable
5. **Commandments** — emphasizing the authority of what is said and the right to give orders
6. **Judgments** (or Ordinances) — related to statutes; God's rulings and decisions
7. **Word** (Hebrew: *dabar*) — the most general term for God's communication of His will to us
8. **Word** (Hebrew: *imrah*) — found in verse 11; refers to God's promises in particular

Every verse except verse 122 includes one of these words. And the interesting thing is that they are all used in approximately equal proportions — about 20 to 25 times each.

## The Structure: An Acrostic Poem

Another thing about the Psalm is that it is structured as an acrostic poem. An acrostic poem is a poem where each line (or in this case each group of verses) starts with one successive letter of the alphabet. There are eight psalms that are acrostic in the Psalter — Psalms 9, 10, 25, 34, 37, 111, 112, and 145. They all use the Hebrew alphabet. But Psalm 119 is unique in that each group of **eight verses** starts with a consecutive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. There are 22 stanzas, one for each of the 22 letters.

You can see this in your Bible: before each section there is a small Hebrew character with its name. The first section (vv. 1-8) is under the letter *Aleph* — the first letter of the Hebrew alphabet — and every one of those eight verses starts with a Hebrew word beginning with *aleph*. The next section (vv. 9-16) is under *Beth*, and every verse starts with *beth*. And so on.

Many believe this was a mnemonic device — something to help you remember what comes next in memorizing the Psalm. But I think there is more. Not just to help memorize it, but also to present the subject in its **exhaustive fullness** — using the whole alphabet, the whole gamut of language. This is the A through Z of the subject, a complete tribute. Just as Jesus calls Himself the Alpha and the Omega in Revelation — the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, the completeness of all things — so the author, inspired by the Holy Spirit, wrote a poem based on every letter of the Hebrew alphabet, saying: I am giving you the A through Z of the Word of God and the God of the Word. It speaks of the perfection, completeness, fullness, finality, and sufficiency of the Word of God.

## The Majesty of the Psalm's Sheer Size

John Piper, writing on how beholding greatness helps us understand ourselves rightly, says: "We are all starved for the glory of God, not self. No one goes to the Grand Canyon to increase self-esteem. Because there is greater healing for the soul in beholding splendor than in beholding self. What could be more ludicrous in a vast and glorious universe than a human being standing in front of a mirror trying to find significance in his own self-image? The Christian gospel is about the glory of Christ, not about me."

Charles Spurgeon wrote about Psalm 119 in his *Treasury of David* — 349 pages on this Psalm alone. He wrote: "I have been bewildered in the expense of the 119th Psalm. Its dimensions and its depth alike overcome me. It spreads itself out before me like a vast rolling prairie to which I can see no bound... This marvelous poem seemed to me a great sea of holy teaching, moving in its many verses wave upon wave altogether without an island. It is a continent of sacred thought, every inch of which is fertile as the garden of the Lord."

Charles Bridges, a 19th-century evangelical preacher, wrote 481 pages of sermons on this Psalm. Most impressive of all is Thomas Manton's three volumes — totaling 1,677 pages, 190 sermons on Psalm 119. Calvin himself preached 22 sermons on it. And Augustine, one of the most brilliant minds in church history, delayed writing on this Psalm until the very end of his work on the Psalms because he said the Psalm always exceeded the powers of his highest thought.

## The Psalm in History

George Wishart, the Bishop of Edinburgh and mentor of John Knox — Knox stood before the pulpit with his claymore drawn, ready to protect Wishart from those who sought to kill him — was eventually condemned to death. When upon the scaffold, Wishart was permitted by tradition to choose a song to be sung. He chose Psalm 119. Before two-thirds of the Psalm was finished, a letter of pardon arrived and his life was spared. The singing of Psalm 119 spared his life that day.

John Newton tells a story of William Grimshaw, an 18th-century minister in England, who would leave the church while the congregation sang before the sermon, walk to the local public houses to find those absent from worship, and "drive them into the church before him." On particularly sparse Sundays, after announcing his text, he would tell the congregation: "Now we're going to sing Psalm 119, all 176 verses." This gave him time to go collect more people and bring them to church.

## The Context: Written in Suffering

This Psalm is the fruit of suffering in the life of its author. There is no title — we do not know for certain who wrote it — but most commentators believe it is post-exilic, perhaps from the time of Ezra and Nehemiah, written in a time of fierce personal persecution.

Verse 23: "Princes also sit and speak against me, but Your servant meditates on Your statutes." Verse 51: "The proud have me in great derision, yet I do not turn aside from Your law." Verse 87: "They almost made an end of me on earth, but I did not forsake Your precepts." Verse 110: "The wicked have laid a snare for me, yet I have not strayed from Your precepts." Verse 157: "Many are my persecutors and my enemies, yet I do not turn from Your testimonies."

This is what was going on in the life of this man who loved God's law. And yet in every verse he turns to the Word of God. God's Word is sufficient not only in the everyday difficulties of life, but in its most extreme difficulties. Verse 143: "Trouble and anguish have overtaken me, yet Your commandments are my delight." Verse 165: "Great peace have those who love Your law, and nothing causes them to stumble." Verse 167: "My soul keeps Your testimonies, and I love them exceedingly."

I want to be able to pray like this man. I want to truly believe and live like I believe these truths. And I pray that is your desire as well, and that our Lord will bless this pursuit as we join our hearts to the Psalmist on this journey.

## Closing Prayer

Father, thank you for your word. We pray that your Spirit would wield it and that beholding your word, he would have his way in our lives. In Jesus' name, amen.
